back: For a square matrix A, a scalar λ is an eigenvalue if there exists a non-zero vector v such that Av = λv. Vector v is called the eigenvector corresponding to λ.
The characteristic equation is |A - λI| = 0, where A is an n×n matrix, λ is the eigenvalue, and I is the identity matrix.
The characteristic polynomial is det(A - λI), which is a polynomial of degree n in λ. Its roots give the eigenvalues of matrix A.
Every square matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation. If p(λ) = det(A - λI) is the characteristic polynomial, then p(A) = 0 (zero matrix).

front: What is the trace in relation to eigenvalues?
back: The sum of eigenvalues equals the trace of the matrix (sum of diagonal elements). Σλᵢ = tr(A)

front: What is the determinant in relation to eigenvalues?
back: The product of eigenvalues equals the determinant of the matrix. Πλᵢ = det(A)
Matrix B is similar to matrix A if B = P⁻¹AP for some invertible matrix P. Similar matrices have the same eigenvalues.
A matrix A is diagonalizable if there exists an invertible matrix P such that P⁻¹AP = D, where D is a diagonal matrix containing eigenvalues.

front: What is a linearly independent eigenvector set?
back: Eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are always linearly independent. An n×n matrix with n distinct eigenvalues is always diagonalizable.
For each eigenvalue λ, solve (A - λI)v = 0 to find the null space. This gives all eigenvectors corresponding to λ.
The eigenspace corresponding to eigenvalue λ is the set of all eigenvectors plus the zero vector, i.e., null space of (A - λI).
Algebraic multiplicity is the multiplicity of λ as a root of characteristic polynomial. Geometric multiplicity is the dimension of eigenspace (number of linearly independent eigenvectors).
A matrix is defective if geometric multiplicity < algebraic multiplicity for some eigenvalue. Such matrices are not diagonalizable.

front: State properties of eigenvalues of special matrices
back: For symmetric matrices, all eigenvalues are real. For orthogonal matrices, eigenvalues have absolute value 1. For skew-symmetric matrices, eigenvalues are purely imaginary or zero.
